topic: A person finds their childhood home listed for sale online
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification flashed across the screen, a jarring splash of unwelcome color against the muted gray of my afternoon. My thumb hovered over the link, a familiar, heavy dread settling in my chest. It was an email, a listing. My childhood home. Sold. The words swam before my eyes, mocking the quiet emptiness of my current apartment. I dragged myself to the window, the city noise a dull roar I couldn't tune out. The house, a ghost from my earliest memories, now a commodity. I leaned my forehead against the cool glass, the breath catching in my throat. Why now? A sudden, sharp ache bloomed behind my ribs. I slowly pulled my hand back from the keyboard, the movement sluggish, as if moving through thick water.

[story 2]

I stared at the listing for what felt like an eternity, the pixels blurring into meaningless shapes. Every muscle in my body felt weighted down, a leaden cloak draped over my shoulders. I reread the description, the familiar details of the overgrown garden and the peeling paint suddenly sharp, slicing through the fog of my current stagnation. A low, shaky sigh escaped my lips, a sound devoid of any real feeling. I pushed back from the desk, the chair scraping loudly against the floor. My gaze drifted to the floorboards, tracing patterns I knew intimately but felt utterly foreign now. It was just another thing to acknowledge, another piece of history being packaged for someone else.

[story 3]

The phone screen illuminated my face, highlighting the dull exhaustion etched around my eyes. I hadn't expected anything, but seeing the address—the address of the place where I learned to ride my bike and scraped my knees—sent a cold tremor through me. My hands clenched into fists, the knuckles turning white against the pale skin. I walked over to the bookshelf, running a fingertip over the spine of a forgotten paperback. The silence in the room seemed amplified, pressing in on me. I didn't move to close the browser tab. Instead, I just stood there, rooted to the spot, feeling a profound sense of inertia, as if the energy to even lift my feet had been completely drained away.
